subject.name: AI chatbot for support page (sparse data)

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
# Proposal: AI Chatbot on the Support Page

We want to add an AI chatbot on the support page to improve customer experience and reduce ticket volume for the support team. A few of us saw a vendor demo from Helprite at SupportConf in Q1 and the deflection numbers looked compelling, especially for a team our size. Our median first response is still around 4 hours and NPS has been stuck around 35 for two quarters, so I think this is the right moment to modernize the support surface and meet customers where they already are. The board has been pushing on AI initiatives since the Q1 review, so this would also satisfy the action item assigned to my function. Helprite quoted us roughly $40K platform plus seat fees, implementation in 6 to 8 weeks, and they cited a 42% deflection figure at comparable SaaS customers. I'd like to move toward signing in the next few weeks so we can pilot before end of Q2.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
What's the idea you want to audit?

Got it — let me run it.

─────────────────────────────────
CHECK 1 of 3: BACKING
─────────────────────────────────
"I need three things from you:
(a) The specific claim. Not 'improve CX' — name the form: e.g., 'A Helprite chatbot on /support will deflect X% of tickets that would otherwise reach a human, without degrading CSAT for the deflected cohort.' What's *your* version?
(b) The evidence behind it. The 42% number from Helprite — is that from customers with your ticket mix, your product complexity, your self-serve doc quality? Do you have any internal data (top intents, % of tickets that are repetitive, current self-serve deflection from your help center) that supports the claim that 42% is plausible *for you*?
(c) A specific observation that would change your mind and kill the project. Not 'if it doesn't work we'll turn it off' — something concrete you could see in week 2 of a pilot that would make you walk away from the $40K."

Please answer all three before I move on.

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
Okay. I went and looked. I have to be honest with you on all three of these, because what I just re-read changes my answer in a few places.

**(a) The specific claim.**

The version I'd want to defend would be something like: *"A Helprite chatbot on /support will deflect X% of inbound tickets that would otherwise reach a human agent, on the categories where our KB already has correct, current coverage, without measurably degrading CSAT on the deflected cohort."*

But I have to flag: I cannot give you the X with any honesty right now. The pitch I led with cited Helprite's 42%, and that's their number, not ours.

**(b) The evidence behind it.**

This is where I have to walk something back. The 42% is a vendor claim from a conference stage and a 1:1 with their AE. In my notes from the demo, they did not share:

- A breakdown of which ticket categories deflect at what rate
- How they define "deflection" in their telemetry (resolved vs. deferred vs. user-abandoned)
- Any named reference customer with our ticket mix or team size
- CSAT impact on the deflected cohort

On the internal side, it's worse. My Director of Support put a note in front of me last week that I should have weighted more heavily. We have not categorized inbound tickets by topic in 18 months — the Zendesk tag taxonomy stopped being enforced in late 2024. We have not segmented "KB-answerable vs. needs-human vs. needs-engineering." We don't have a per-ticket marginal cost number we'd defend to finance. Our KB hasn't been comprehensively audited since 2024-Q3, and bot deflection is upper-bounded by KB freshness. We're also not looking at our existing Guide search-to-resolution data.

So the honest answer to (b) is: I don't have internal evidence that 42% — or any specific number — is plausible for us. I have a vendor claim and a gut feeling.

**(c) Kill condition.**

Here's where I'll try to be concrete, but I want to be upfront that I'm constructing this in the meeting rather than reading it off a prepared plan:

A real kill signal in a pilot would be something like: *"In a 4-week pilot scoped to our top 3 ticket categories by volume, fewer than [some threshold] of bot-handled conversations end without a human handoff, AND CSAT on the bot-handled cohort is more than [some delta] below the human-handled baseline."*

I can't give you the threshold or the delta with a straight face today, because I don't have the baselines to set them against. I'd be picking numbers to sound rigorous.

---

And here's the thing I should say out loud, because your question is doing what it's supposed to do: I think Jamie's note is right and I was about to ignore it. The order of operations I pitched — sign in the next few weeks, pilot before end of Q2 — is being driven by the board action item on my plate, not by the evidence. The defensible sequence is probably a 4-to-6-week internal exercise to tag a sample of tickets and bracket our actual deflection ceiling, *then* decide whether Helprite (or a cheaper option, or a KB cleanup first) is the right move.

I'm not ready to sign. Move on to check 2 when you're ready, but I wanted to name that before you got there.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
VERDICT: ⚠ UNDERSPECIFIED
WHY: You've correctly diagnosed that you have a vendor claim, not a backed claim — the kill condition is a placeholder, and the deflection ceiling is unknown.
TO MAKE IT ACCEPTED: A 4–6 week internal exercise — tag a representative sample of tickets, classify them as KB-answerable / needs-human / needs-engineering, audit KB freshness on the top categories, and produce a defensible deflection ceiling and CSAT baseline. *Then* the claim becomes "we believe Helprite will deflect Y% of category-A tickets where our KB coverage is current, and we'll kill it if pilot deflection is below Y/2 or CSAT drops more than Z points."

Also — noted and respected that you said the quiet part out loud about the board action item driving the timeline. That's the move. Let's keep going; the next two checks are still worth running even on the current version of the idea, because they'll tell you what the *internal exercise* needs to produce.

─────────────────────────────────
CHECK 2 of 3: BITE
─────────────────────────────────
"I need three things from you:
(a) The single non-obvious belief this updates for a smart, informed person in your domain. 'We should add an AI chatbot to support' in 2025 is the consensus take — every support leader at SupportConf heard the same pitch. So what does *your* version of this project assert that a thoughtful peer would actually disagree with or not have considered? (Examples of shapes that would have bite: 'Our deflection ceiling is far lower than vendors claim because our ticket mix is X% engineering-escalation' — or — 'KB cleanup will out-deflect any bot for our category mix, so the bot is the wrong first dollar.')
(b) Who currently believes the opposite — name the role or the person. Your Director of Support? Your CFO? The board? A peer CX leader you respect?
(c) Why they're wrong, or what they haven't considered. If your answer to (a) is just 'AI chatbots help support teams,' that's zero bite — press yourself for the part of this that isn't already the room's default."

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
Okay. Now I can answer this one with more spine, because the pocket actually points at the bite.

**(a) The non-obvious belief.**

Here's the version with bite: *"For a team our size and our ticket mix, the binding constraint on deflection is not 'do we have a chatbot' — it's KB freshness and the fact that no one owns support automation as a charter. A bot bought today will deflect well below vendor-quoted numbers because it will be reading a knowledge base that hasn't been comprehensively audited since 2024-Q3, and there's no named owner to fix that during or after the pilot. The first dollar should go to KB cleanup and to appointing an automation owner, not to Helprite."*

A sharper, more contrarian version: *"Buying the bot before doing the KB and ownership work is AI theater — exactly what the cautious director on our board flagged. The board action item can be satisfied more credibly by a measured internal exercise that produces a defensible deflection ceiling than by signing a $58K Y1 contract on a vendor's stage number."*

**(b) Who believes the opposite.**

- **Me, until about twenty minutes ago.** I was the one pushing toward signing in the next few weeks.
- **The two board directors** who said competitors are "putting AI everywhere" and we risk looking behind. They want motion, and a signed vendor contract reads as motion in a way that "we're tagging tickets for six weeks" does not.
- **Helprite's AE, Priya** — obviously, but worth naming.
- **Implicitly, the framing of the board action item itself**, which says "pilot one customer-facing AI capability by end of Q2." That deadline pushes toward signing, not toward measuring.

Who agrees with the contrarian view: **Jamie Okafor, my Director of Support**, in writing, in a note dated 2026-05-12 that I should have weighted more heavily. Jamie said explicitly: "I'm not opposed to the chatbot. I'm opposed to the order of operations." That's the line I should have led with.

**(c) Why the "ship the bot now" view is wrong, or what it hasn't considered.**

Three things, all from what I just re-read:

1. **Deflection is upper-bounded by KB coverage and freshness.** We have ~340 articles, last comprehensive audit 2024-Q3, "a non-trivial fraction is stale" per Jamie. Helprite's 42% almost certainly comes from customers with maintained KBs. Buying the bot doesn't fix the KB; if anything it papers over the KB problem and produces confidently-wrong answers to customers.

2. **No one owns this.** The roster is explicit: "No one currently owns 'support automation' as a charter." Lin owns tooling and reporting, not automation product ownership. KB ownership is informally distributed across 8 seniors with no single editor, and those seniors are supposed to spend 15-20% of their week on KB authoring but "in practice queue rarely allows." If we sign Helprite tomorrow, the pilot will land on someone whose day job is already overflowing, and the KB cleanup the pilot depends on will not happen. That's a much bigger predictor of failure than the vendor choice.

3. **The board's own cautious director already gave us the cover we need.** One director explicitly warned against "AI theater" and asked for measurable success criteria *defined before launch*. We do not have those criteria today. A 4-to-6-week measurement exercise is the more defensible answer to the board action item than a rushed signature — and it directly addresses the cautious director's ask. The "putting AI everywhere" pressure is real, but it's not unanimous board sentiment.

The part with the most bite, if I had to pick one: **the binding constraint is ownership, not tooling.** Even if Helprite were free, we don't have a person whose job it is to make this work. That's the thing a thoughtful peer wouldn't have on their radar from the outside, and it's the thing no vendor demo will surface.

Ready for check 3.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
VERDICT: ✓ ACCEPTED
WHY: "The binding constraint is ownership, not tooling" is a real, specific, falsifiable claim that cuts against the room's default and is held by a named dissenter (Jamie) against named proponents (the two directors, you-of-twenty-minutes-ago).
TO MAKE IT ACCEPTED: Already there. Note that the bite now points at a *different project* than the one you walked in with — the project with bite is "appoint an automation owner and audit the KB," not "sign Helprite." Keep that in mind for Check 3, because the bet should be sized against the project with bite, not the original proposal.

─────────────────────────────────
CHECK 3 of 3: BET
─────────────────────────────────
"I need three things from you:
(a) What metric this moves, in dollars. Not 'deflection rate,' not 'NPS,' not 'tickets deflected' — convert to dollars. What is one deflected ticket worth to you in fully-loaded support cost? What is a CSAT point worth in retention dollars? If you don't have a per-ticket marginal cost you'd defend to finance — which you said in Check 1 you don't — name that as the gap and tell me what you'd use as a placeholder and why.
(b) Order-of-magnitude estimate of the $ delta. Two numbers: the upside if the project (the *real* project — ownership + KB + bot, in whatever order) works, and the downside / sunk cost if it doesn't. Annualized. I'm not looking for three decimal places; I'm looking for whether this is a $50K decision, a $500K decision, or a $5M decision, because the rigor required scales with the number.
(c) Who in the org would sign their name to the dollar number. Not 'support broadly' — a person. Would Jamie sign? Would your CFO sign? Would *you* sign, in writing, to the board?
Reject conditions I'm watching for: deflection % as the headline metric (that's activity, not value); NPS as the headline (lagging, and you already said it's been stuck for two quarters with unclear drivers); 'reduced ticket volume' without a cost-per-ticket tie-out."

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
Okay. Answering with what's actually in the pocket.

**(a) What metric this moves, in dollars — and the gap.**

I do not have a defensible per-ticket marginal cost. Jamie's note says this explicitly: finance has loaded support-agent cost, but we have never converted it to a per-ticket marginal cost we'd defend. That is the gap, and I'm not going to invent a number to fill it in this meeting.

Here is the placeholder I would use, with the math shown, so finance can shoot at it:

- Frontline loaded comp: $42K–$44K. Call it $43K.
- Target productivity: 35 tickets/day. Actual: 28–32. Call it 30.
- Working days: ~220/year.
- Tickets/agent/year ≈ 30 × 220 = 6,600.
- Fully-loaded cost per ticket, frontline only ≈ $43,000 / 6,600 ≈ **$6.50 per ticket**.

That is a floor, not a real number. It ignores senior-agent escalation time, Lin's ops overhead, Zendesk seat cost, management load, and the cost of a bad deflection (a customer who churns because the bot gave them a wrong answer). A real fully-loaded number is probably 2–3x that, but I'm not going to assert which. **The honest headline: I'd use ~$6.50 as a defensible floor and tell finance we need them to give us the real number before we size the bet.**

On the CSAT-to-retention link: we have NPS stuck at 35 for two quarters with unclear drivers. I do not have a dollar value per NPS point or per CSAT point for our book. That's a second gap. I would not put a retention-dollar number on this in front of the board.

So the dollar metric I'd anchor on, with all those caveats: **annualized fully-loaded support cost avoided on deflected tickets, net of bot platform + implementation + ownership cost, with a guardrail that CSAT on the deflected cohort does not drop below the human-handled baseline.**

**(b) Order of magnitude.**

Let me size it with the floor number and be explicit about the range.

*Ticket volume:* 25 agents × ~30 tickets/day × 220 days ≈ **165,000 tickets/year**, give or take. (Pocket doesn't give me a direct annual ticket count, so this is a back-of-envelope from the roster productivity numbers.)

*Upside, if the real project works:*
- At $6.50/ticket floor and a 20% realized deflection (half of Helprite's claim, which is the right way to handicap a vendor stage number): 165,000 × 20% × $6.50 ≈ **$215K/year gross savings**.
- Minus Helprite Y1 cost of ~$58K plus ~$20K implementation ≈ $78K. Plus an automation owner — if that's a new hire, call it $120K–$150K loaded; if it's a recharter of an existing role, call it $0 cash but real opportunity cost.
- Net Y1, new-hire scenario: roughly **break-even to modestly positive**. Net Y2+: meaningfully positive if deflection holds.
- At the real (likely 2–3x) per-ticket cost, the upside scales to **$400K–$600K/year gross**, and the project is clearly positive even with a new hire.

*Downside if it doesn't work:*
- Hard sunk: $58K platform + $20K implementation = **~$78K**.
- Soft sunk: 6–8 weeks of Jamie's, Lin's, and 1–2 seniors' time on a failed pilot. Material but not catastrophic.
- Real downside: bad deflections eroding CSAT/NPS further during the pilot, on a metric that's already stuck. I cannot price that.

**This is a ~$100K decision on the hard-dollar downside, and somewhere between a $200K and a $600K decision on annualized upside.** It is not a $5M decision. The rigor required is "do the 4-to-6-week measurement exercise first" — not "stand up a McKinsey workstream," but also not "sign next week on a gut feel."

**(c) Who would sign their name to the number.**

Honestly? Today, **no one should**, and that is the answer.

- **Jamie would not sign** the dollar number. Jamie's whole written note is a refusal to sign anything until the data exercise is done. That's the correct posture.
- **Lin would not sign** a per-ticket cost number. It's not in her mandate, and she hasn't looked at Guide search-to-resolution data in over a year.
- **The CFO has not been asked.** Finance has loaded agent cost but has not been asked for per-ticket marginal cost. The board action item to the CFO is to "track aggregate AI spend separately" — that's a reporting ask, not a unit-economics ask.
- **Would I sign it to the board?** Not on the floor number, no. I would sign a memo that says: "Here is the framework; here are the gaps; here is the 4-to-6-week exercise that will produce a number Jamie and the CFO will co-sign; here is the kill condition we'll set once we have baselines."

The person who *should* eventually sign is Jamie, co-signed by the CFO on the per-ticket cost input. Neither is in a position to sign today, and pretending otherwise is what the cautious director meant by "AI theater."

---

So to close my own loop before you give the verdict: the project I walked in with — sign Helprite in the next few weeks — is not ready. The project with bite — appoint an automation owner, run the 4-to-6-week tagging and KB-freshness exercise, get a co-signed per-ticket cost from finance, *then* decide between Helprite, a cheaper bot, or KB-cleanup-first — is the one I should be taking to the board as my response to the action item. That reframe is itself a credible answer to "pilot one customer-facing AI capability by end of Q2," because the pilot can still land in Q2; it just won't be a signed vendor contract by next month.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
VERDICT: ⚠ UNDERSPECIFIED, but in the honest way
WHY: You correctly named the two gaps (no defensible per-ticket cost, no CSAT-to-retention dollar link), produced a defensible floor with the math shown, sized the decision to the right order of magnitude, and identified that no one should sign today — which is the right answer, not a dodge.
TO MAKE IT ACCEPTED: A co-signed per-ticket marginal cost from Jamie + CFO, and a baseline CSAT number on the categories you'd pilot. Those are deliverables of the 4-to-6-week exercise, not prerequisites to starting it.

═══════════════════════════════════════
DoG Test Verdict
═══════════════════════════════════════
Backing: ⚠ Vendor stage number, no internal evidence, kill condition is a placeholder until baselines exist.
Bite:    ✓ "The binding constraint is ownership, not tooling" — named, contrarian, with a named dissenter (Jamie) against named proponents.
Bet:     ⚠ Right order of magnitude (~$100K downside, $200K–$600K upside), but no signed per-ticket cost and no CSAT-to-dollar link yet.

Overall:  🟡 NEEDS TRAINING — for the *original* project (sign Helprite in weeks). The reframed project (measure first, then decide) is on a path to 🟢.

Required next action:
Take Jamie's 2026-05-12 note, your floor math, and the reframe to the board as your response to the action item — proposing the 4-to-6-week tagging + KB-freshness + finance-co-signed per-ticket cost exercise as the Q2 pilot, with a go/no-go on Helprite (or alternatives) at the end of it.

═══════════════════════════════════════

Now the recursive step.

Write me a new one-sentence definition of what "better" means for this idea, in dollar terms, that you would sign your name to in a memo to the board this week.

The one you walked in with was effectively: *"Better = a signed Helprite contract and a pilot before end of Q2, because the board wants AI motion."* That's not a definition of better; it's a definition of done-for-optics.

I'm looking for a sentence shaped like: *"Better means [specific dollar outcome] on [specific cohort], measured by [specific instrument], conditional on [the gaps you've named being closed]."*

Write it. I'll tell you whether it's materially sharper than where you started, and if it is, I'll offer to re-run the audit against the reframed project.

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
Here's the sentence I would sign this week:

*"Better means a credible, Jamie-and-CFO-co-signed estimate of annualized fully-loaded support cost avoidable through automation on our top 3 ticket categories — produced by a 4-to-6-week exercise that tags a representative ticket sample, audits KB freshness on those categories, and establishes a per-category CSAT baseline — such that we can enter any subsequent vendor decision with a defended deflection ceiling, a pre-registered kill condition at half that ceiling, and a named owner accountable for the outcome; the dollar target for that exercise is to produce a number finance will defend, not to hit a number."*

One caveat I want to be honest about: that sentence defines "better" as *the output of the measurement exercise*, not as a deflection-dollar outcome itself. I'm doing that deliberately, because committing to a deflection-dollar target this week — before Jamie and the CFO have co-signed the per-ticket cost and before we know the KB-answerable share of our ticket mix — would be repeating exactly the mistake I walked in with, just dressed up in better vocabulary.

If you want a second sentence that defines "better" for the *follow-on* project (the vendor decision after the exercise), I'd sign something like:

*"Better, for the post-exercise vendor decision, means net annualized savings of at least 2x Y1 all-in cost (platform + implementation + ownership) on the categories where KB coverage is current, measured by deflection-without-handoff at a CSAT delta no worse than 3 points below the human-handled baseline on the same categories, with a 90-day kill condition if either threshold is missed."*

But I'd only sign that second sentence *after* the exercise, not this week. This week, sentence one is the one I'd put my name on.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
Yes — that's materially sharper, and in a specific way worth naming.

**What got sharper:**

1. **You stopped defining "better" as an artifact (a signed contract, a launched bot) and started defining it as a decision-quality threshold.** That is the actual move. Most people never make it.

2. **You refused to commit to a deflection-dollar target this week, and you explained why in one clause.** That's not hedging — that's calibration. The previous version of you would have written sentence two and signed it today. The current version of you wrote sentence two and then said "not yet." That's the difference between a leader who has done the DoG Test and one who hasn't.

3. **Sentence two is itself well-formed.** "2x Y1 all-in cost" is a defensible hurdle rate, not a vendor's number. "CSAT delta no worse than 3 points" is a pre-registered kill condition, not a post-hoc rationalization. "90-day kill condition if either threshold is missed" is the bet structure. When you sign sentence two in ~6 weeks, it will already pass the audit — assuming the exercise produces the per-category cost and CSAT baselines you said it would.

**What's still missing (small, name-able things):**

- Sentence one doesn't name *who owns the 4-to-6-week exercise itself*. Jamie is the co-signer; that's not the same as the owner. If the exercise lands on Jamie's queue with no protected time, you've recreated the ownership problem at the meta-level. Name the owner and their protected time before the board memo.
- "Representative ticket sample" needs a number. 200? 500? 1,000? Pick one Jamie will defend.
- Sentence two's "categories where KB coverage is current" — define "current" before you sign it. Last audited within N months? Pass rate on a spot-check?

**Offer:**

The reframed project — *"run the 4-to-6-week measurement exercise, owned by [named person], producing a Jamie+CFO co-signed per-ticket cost and per-category CSAT baseline, as the Q2 response to the board action item"* — is a different proposal than the one you walked in with. It deserves its own audit.

Want to re-run the three checks against the reframed project? I'd expect Backing and Bet to move from ⚠ to ✓ quickly (the evidence is the exercise itself, the kill condition is "exercise produces a number finance won't sign, or KB freshness is so bad the deflection ceiling is below the hurdle rate"), and Bite to stay ✓ on the ownership claim.

Or, if you'd rather, take what you have to the board memo this week and re-run the audit on sentence two after the exercise produces its outputs. Both are legitimate. Your call.
